subject: Staves and quarterstaves
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 062 (not the heuristic)
note: All 29 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly (the transcript's auto-caption 'F tier' for Staff of Cherished Necromancy is a slip for 'S tier' — context is unambiguous). The 'why' field is new content. +1 spell save DC is the prized stat; many casters dual-wield staves. Auto-caption renders Markoheshkir as 'marah hesar' and Corellon's Grace as 'kellan's/corin's grace'.

Corellon's Grace.why: A magical quarterstaff (no enhancement) granting +1 unarmed attack/damage and, while unarmored, +2 to all saves — excellent early for monks who haven't taken (or won't take) Tavern Brawler, offering both offense and strong early-game defense.

Melf's First Staff.why: +1 quarterstaff with +1 spell save DC and spell attack rolls (the single most important caster stat) plus a free Melf's Acid Arrow; best-in-slot for most spellcasters across all of Acts 1-2.

Mourning Frost.why: +1d4+1 cold damage on hit, makes enemies Chilled (cold-vulnerable, enabling freeze-stuns with Wet), boosts all your cold damage, and grants Ray of Frost at will — the premier cold-damage staff, best-in-slot for cold builds and a common off-hand all game.

Markoheshkir.why: The only legendary staff: +1 save DC/attack, a free 6th-level spell once/day (Arcane Battery), AND elemental attunement (lightning gives Chain Lightning once/short rest) — best-in-slot for essentially every spellcaster; builds are designed around maximizing it.

Staff of Cherished Necromancy.why: +2 staff, +1d4 necrotic, enemies have disadvantage vs your necromancy, and killing with a spell grants a free necromancy spell — a self-sustaining engine of free 6th-level casts, the only staff competitive with Markoheshkir (great dual-wielded with it).

Staff of Spellpower.why: A strictly-worse Markoheshkir (no attunement) — but still busted: +1 save DC/attack and a free spell once/day; an extra 6th-level cast daily for any spellcaster, used to Nova or to bank a day-long buff.

Staff of Arcane Blessing.why: Free Bless once/rest plus, when you Bless, an extra +1d4 to spell attack rolls (the tooltip lies that the whole bonus is additive); excellent for any party making frequent spell attacks (Eldritch Blast, Scorching Ray, cantrips).

The Spellsparkler.why: Grants 2 lightning charges whenever you deal spell/weapon damage (+1 to hit, +1 lightning/instance, 1d8 at 5 stacks); great on multi-attack casters like warlocks and fire sorcerers, and scales up as they fire more beams late-game.

Caitiff Staff.why: +2 staff with +1 save DC/attack and restores an expended warlock spell slot once/rest; a strict upgrade over Melf's for warlocks (and Sorlock sorcery-point fuel), plus a solid +save-DC off-hand for dual-wield staff builds.

Staff of Interruption.why: Counterspell at level 5 once/rest — lets a non-caster (e.g. an archer) carry the game's best defensive reaction; a great safety swap-in against enemy casters and a fine off-hand for any spellcaster. Underrated.

Staff of the Emperor.why: +2 staff, +1 save DC/attack, and whenever you succeed on a save the caster must pass an Intelligence save (a weak save) or be stunned — free, action-less stuns; superb on a high-AC/high-save character enemies can only target with spells.

Quarterstaff.why: The base non-magical staff: two-handed d8 is the highest-damage simple weapon (fire-dippable), and Topple knocks prone — a surprisingly good melee pick for simple-weapon characters in the very early game, swapped out ASAP.

Cacophony.why: +1 staff with +1d4 thunder and Thunderous Smite once/rest; a reasonable weapon for an attacking monk (though Smite competes with Flurry of Blows) and an early thunder source for reverberation builds — situational.

Gold Wyrmling Staff.why: +1 staff, +1d4 fire (doesn't stack with fire-dipping, so no real damage gain), but Firebolt at will is a genuine cantrip upgrade for Druids/clerics/bards lacking good damage cantrips — a fine early pick for a melee-capable caster like a Spore Druid.

Incandescent Staff.why: +1 ranged spell attack, fire resistance, Firebolt at will, and Fireball once/rest; the spell-attack bonus is beaten by Melf's/Spellsparkler, but the free Fireball is a worthwhile swap-in for one Act 2 encounter, or a decent off-hand staff.

Hollow's Staff.why: +1 staff, +1d4 necrotic, and disadvantage on enemy saves vs your necromancy; mostly just lands more damage spells, but pairs well with a debuff like Eyebite — generously B for an Eyebite-focused build.

Woe.why: +2 staff, +1 save DC/attack, free Blight once/rest, and 1d4 heal whenever an enemy fails a save vs your spells; a fine all-rounder, but essentially the fifth-best of the +save-DC staffs, outclassed by Markoheshkir/Cherished Necromancy/Emperor.

Nature's Snare.why: Hitting an enemy forces a DC12 strength save or Ensnared (a strong lockdown condition); used to apply on any damage you dealt (a great bug, now fixed), so as a no-enhancement staff it's hard to land — only for a themed ensnaring build.

Pale Oak.why: No enhancement; immunity to druidic Vines and Entangle once/rest — both weak effects, since few enemies entangle you; a generous C for a niche build that stands inside its own Entangle.

Despair of Athkatla.why: +2 staff with +1 save DC/attack — fine, but it's the worst and hardest-to-get of the many staves granting that effect, so only relevant if you run multiple casters who all want it (e.g. an all-wizard party).

Harper Sacredstriker.why: +1 staff with a level-6 Spiritual Weapon once/rest; the upcast (3d8/turn, bonus action) is okay damage but Act 3 enemies are too mobile and fights too short for it to do much — a marginal swap-in or second weapon.

Staff of the Ram.why: +2 staff that once/turn forces a DC8 con save (surely bugged-low) or knockback + stun; at this point enemies auto-pass DC8 even with disadvantage, so you'd need heavy reverberation stacking, and it costs you a real weapon's damage.

+1 Quarterstaff.why: A generic +1 staff; staff-wielding casters rarely attack with it, and the few who do have far better weapon options even from the start — no reason to buy it.

Creation's Echo.why: No enhancement; grants resistance to the elemental damage type you just dealt (not cold), but you must trigger it, it's purely defensive, and it eats your Elixir slot — just stockpile resistance elixirs instead.

Rain Dancer.why: No enhancement unless you cast Create Water once/rest — an effect a bottle of water duplicates, so spending a weapon slot (that you must buy) on it isn't worth it; mainly for camp showers.

Staff of Crones.why: No enhancement unless you cast Ray of Sickness once/rest — a weak level-1 spell that's usually worse than whatever else you'd do, and the staff cripples your stats otherwise; a grenade or scroll beats it.

Bigby's Chew Toy.why: +1 staff with Enlarge once/rest; a level-2 spell is unexciting in Act 2, and a +1 quarterstaff isn't a weapon the enlarge-wanting attacker actually wants — no real use case.

+2 Quarterstaff.why: A generic +2 staff (Act 3); you aren't attacking with staves by then, and the characters who do are better off with Mourning Frost — same verdict as the +1 generic.

Staff of a Mumbling Wizard.why: A joke item: 'Firebolt?' at will (always 1d10, 1-in-20 chance to be a Fireball, and counts as an enemy failed-save 19/20 times for odd triggers); the weak base spell makes none of the quirky interactions worth your action.
